summary: O'Neil recounts refusing to switch airplane seats so an orthodox Jewish man wouldn't have to sit near women, connecting this incident to a broader personal transformation. She describes how her recent loss of desire to have more children fundamentally changed her relationship with men—she no longer unconsciously accommodates them or forgives offensive behavior from attractive people. The piece is a candid reflection on how biological drives she wasn't even aware of had been shaping her social interactions for years. She concludes that this hormonal shift has made her more clear-eyed about people, judging them by what they say rather than their charm or attractiveness.
key_points: Refusing to accommodate sexist religious practices on an airplane prompted deeper self-reflection about accommodation patterns
The desire for children created an unconscious bias toward forgiving and accommodating attractive men
Losing that biological drive felt like losing 'a sense of smell' that had been distorting her perception of people
She now evaluates people based on their words and ideas rather than their charm or physical appeal
The change is described as subtle but profound—she's 'immune to the charm of beautiful people' while still appreciating wit, intelligence, and empathy
money_quotes: I think it has to do with the fact that I no longer want to have children.
There was something in my head that I didn't even recognize until it was gone, something that made me size up every man I met as a potential baby daddy.
They honestly could get away with saying dumb or even offensive things around me and I'd instantly forgive them. I didn't even notice doing it. It's like I was drunk on their youth.
The moment I stopped wanting kids, I stopped accommodating men.
It's like I've lost a sense of smell, but one that distracted me from observing people in other ways.
I have become immune to the charm of beautiful people.
To me it's unreasonable to interrupt a bunch of women's lives to accommodate a man, especially when we're trying to get some goddamn work done.
key_insight: Unconscious biological drives can shape our social behavior and judgments in ways we don't recognize until those drives disappear.
